The Light and Shadow of Frontend Engineering: Think It Through Before You Switch In

We're in an age where information is everywhere and easy to grab — which means we have to spend more energy thinking, comparing, and deciding. Especially when "becoming a developer" is the trendy career switch. This post is the honest version of what frontend engineering looks like — the upside (salary ceiling, opportunities, flexibility) and the downside (information anxiety, communication load, the reality of "remote" and "freedom") — so you can make an informed decision.
Windows "Could Not Find This Item" — When a File or Folder Refuses to Delete

Recently hit a familiar Windows annoyance — try to delete a file or folder, and Windows says "Could not find this item". I've been seeing this since Windows 7, and it's still around on Windows 10. This post covers the two common causes (a process holding the file open vs. disk sector trouble) and the easy ways to fix each.
Productivity Boost: Automate Google Forms, Sheets, and Calendar With GAS

Google Forms and Google Calendar are tools we use constantly, but copying form submissions into the calendar by hand is the kind of busywork that screams "automate me". This post walks through using Google Apps Script (GAS) to wire Forms → Sheets → Calendar end to end — reading sheet data, parsing the locale-dependent time format, avoiding duplicate event creation, and triggering the script on a 10-minute schedule.
Maybe You Should Know a Bit About CI/CD: Deploying to Render.com with CD

Knowing a bit about CI/CD is genuinely useful as a developer — even on a side project, you can put it to work. This post covers what CI/CD actually is, the YAML basics behind GitHub Actions, how workflows are structured (Event → Runner → Job → Step), and a full walkthrough of building a PR-check workflow (ESLint + Jest) and a deploy workflow that ships to Render.com via Deploy Hooks.
Demo Day and Your Service Fell Asleep? Use UptimeRobot or cron-job.org to Keep It Awake

Side projects on free hosting tiers tend to sleep after periods of inactivity — and the wake-up delay is the worst when you're trying to demo something. This post covers how to keep a free Render.com (or similar) service awake using UptimeRobot or cron-job.org, with the full setup walkthrough for both — what URL to monitor, what interval to pick, and which one I personally end up using.
Common Discord Problems and How to Fix Them
A grab-bag of common Discord issues — can't log in, can't accept invites, no audio, missing DMs from strangers, and more — with fixes for each. I'll keep adding to this list as I run into new ones.
How to Draw a Flow Chart From Scratch (Symbols + Tools + Worked Example)

Flow charts are great for untangling how a feature actually works. This post walks through the basic symbols, my favourite drawing tools, and a real example: charting out a user registration flow from start to finish.
Use ChatGPT Without Paying or Writing Code (2023 Snapshot)

Heads up — this post is from April 2023, written when ChatGPT was newly available and people weren't sure how to register. The signup flow described here has changed since (it's much simpler now), and the noted "data cutoff is 2021" is also outdated as newer models have launched. The prompting tips section still applies. Worth keeping as a snapshot of early ChatGPT adoption.
Replace Mac's Built-In Spotlight With Raycast

Raycast is a Mac app that can fully replace Spotlight — and it's way more capable. This post walks through installing Raycast, swapping Spotlight's Cmd+Space hotkey over to Raycast, the basic features (open apps, calculator), setting per-app aliases, using Raycast as a Google search bar, the clipboard history feature, and installing extensions like Google Translate from the Raycast Store.
Beyond Render and Fly: Zeabur Is Worth a Look

After Heroku killed its free tier, every cloud platform rushed in with free offerings to scoop up those users. Render.com and Fly.io are the usual recommendations, but there's another one worth knowing about — Zeabur, a deployment platform that bills itself on simplicity and "one-click" deploys. This post walks through signing up, deploying an Express app from GitHub, and customizing the domain.
